general.title: 1. General system overview
general.content:
The program is designed for planning and tracking tasks within an autonomous team managed by a master (team leader). It allows creating process maps (tech cards) for each order, distributing work among performers, taking into account their qualifications and workload, and controlling the execution of operations. This approach helps optimize production processes and minimize emergencies.

All planning in the program takes place in the future. It is impossible to schedule work for yesterday. The planning horizon is 90 days ahead, and plans can be edited within this period. The program also stores the execution history for 90 days in the past. After this period, data about the cards and their execution process is deleted.

The planning process looks like this:

The master creates a process map (tech card), which logically corresponds to a client order. In the card, they describe the final product, the sequence of operations with their specifics, and the necessary materials. At this stage, performers are not yet assigned — the card is a plan that will be executed in the future.

A plan is a fixed sequence of logically connected operations (in the program — actions), each of which is described in the team’s action catalog and has an individual duration, as well as input materials and a final output result.

When the tech card is fully prepared (status PREPARED), you go to the planning tab and drag & drop the card onto the planning field. The program will automatically schedule the card for execution: it will select performers (called units in the system) and distribute the work among them in such a way as to complete the entire card as early as possible, taking into account already scheduled tasks from other cards.

Performers are selected based on their qualification level (each unit has settings indicating which operations they perform and with what time coefficient).

Less experienced performers are given extra time according to their settings (operation time coefficient). The master can make manual adjustments if necessary to redistribute the work.

After the plan is finally approved by the master, each task goes into the individual schedule of the performer. There it contains exact instructions: which materials to use, what to do, and what result to achieve. The calculated execution time is also indicated.

When the work is completed, the performer presses the “Done” button. If the team has a controller, the task goes to them for review. The controller either confirms the result or marks the operation as defective. If there is no controller, the performer themselves indicates whether the task was completed successfully or with defects.

Each tech card goes through a set of states:

  • DRAFT — draft, where the plan is created;
  • PREPARED — ready for task distribution;
  • PLANED — tasks distributed among performers;
  • PERFORMED — tasks completed and awaiting review;
  • READY — product fully produced;
  • DEFECTED — defects found, additional operations required for correction;
  • CLOSED — card finished;
  • CANCELED — card canceled.

Thus, the master always sees the current picture of what is happening, can manage the flow of tasks, adjust the plan, and quickly respond to emerging problems.

cards.title: 2. Process maps
cards.content:
Process map is the foundation of planning in the system. It describes the final result, the sequence of production operations (called actions in the program), and the materials used. The map is created before task distribution and allows you to precisely define what needs to be done.

2.1. Sections of the process map

Catalog — a list of all materials, products, and intermediate semi-finished goods. This list exists only within the process map and is intended for classifying material items as well as synchronizing them with other systems through the sync field. When the list is created, the program assigns its own sync code, but you can use your own.

Products — the list of final results. It corresponds to the order and is always filled in first. Until actions are added, the program assumes that the products come from the warehouse.

Production actions — each action has input items (sources), a description of the work, a result, and estimated time. The program automatically determines the source (from the warehouse) if it is not specified manually. Actions can be linked by dragging the result of one action into the source of another.

Waste — the system allows specifying waste. This is done by dragging an object into the waste area. Important: the system does not perform warehouse accounting — materials and products are indicated for reference only.

Stages — actions can be grouped by stages. This is only a visual division.

2.2. Codes

Each element is assigned a code:

  • P — product
  • A — action
  • W — waste
  • M — material
  • O — action result

Examples:

  • P26|A51O26 — product 26 obtained as a result of action 51
  • P26|M26 — product 26 taken from the warehouse
  • W25|A51O25 — waste 25 obtained from action 51
  • M22 — material 22 required for the order

This labeling makes it possible to accurately track the origin of each item and understand where the required materials and products should come from for each action input.

2.3. Templates

You can save a map as a template if orders are often repeated. The button for saving a map as a template is located on the top menu bar of the map. The template can then be used when creating a new map. Template management is available in the section Resources → Templates.

2.4. Map storage

Process maps are stored in the system for 90 days. After that, they are automatically deleted. If necessary, you can export a map to a .json file and later upload it back through the “Upload JSON” interface.

plan.title: 3. Planning
plan.content:
3.1. Planning Panel Areas

Left Panel
The left panel displays active tech cards:

  • Planned — cards with statuses planed, performed, ready;
  • Prepared — cards with status prepared;
  • For Correction — cards with status defected.

Right Panel
Here is a timeline covering the range from -90 days to +90 days from the current date. The timeline displays the workload schedule of units, considering their individual calendars. You can move the timeline by holding the right mouse button.

3.2. Status Assignment Principle

Status is determined by the youngest (unfilled) operation in the card.

  • Draft — the initial status of a new card. After editing, the master clicks “Send for planning,” and the status changes to Prepared.
  • Prepared — the card is ready for planning. You can drag it to the timeline, and the system will automatically schedule tasks. Manual adjustment of unit or time is allowed. After saving, the status changes to Planed.
  • Planed — tasks appear in the performers’ interfaces. The unit executes the task and presses “Completed,” “Ready,” or “Defect.”

Features:

  • If an operation is non-interruptible, it must be performed without interruption. The program will move it to the nearest suitable interval or display an error.
  • If an operation is interruptible, it can be split across intervals and performed with breaks.
  • Before each operation, the system may automatically insert a setup interval.

During execution, the master sees the interval change color on the timeline. If a task is completed with defects, the card switches to Defected status and appears in the “For Correction” panel.

After receiving the Ready status, the master can close the card, removing it from the planning interface.

Context Menu: accessible via right-click on each interval. You can cancel the planning of a single operation (eraser) or the entire card.

defects.title: 4. Correction of Defects
defects.content:
Replanning Principle

When a performer creates a defective result, the master sees that a certain action is marked in red. The card appears in the “For Correction” list in the planning panel.

To correct the defect, the master must return to editing the card. On the action marked as “defect,” a “Correct” button appears. Clicking it creates an exact copy of the action marked as “correction” and linked to the original defective action. The new action must be edited, saved, and sent for planning — effectively repeating the entire cycle, but for the corrective operation.

The card must also be replanned: dragged to the planning field, saved, and switched to planed status. The additional operation will appear in the performer’s interface and will be completed accordingly.

If it is necessary to add extra actions during correction, this can be done until the card reaches the ready status.

mohitor.title: 5. Task Execution and Monitoring
mohitor.content:
5.1. Monitor

The master can view all task panels of their units for control. Each day, the unit comes to work, opens a link, and sees their current task: which order, what needs to be done, what materials to use, and the deadline. All items in the task have their own code.

When the unit completes the task, they indicate it by pressing the “Completed” button on their phone.

5.2. Card Readiness

The program includes a “Card Readiness” report, which shows the planned completion time of the card (order) based on the team’s current workload.

5.3. Unit KPI

The program includes a “Unit KPI” report, which shows the workload of each unit, defect percentage, and percentage of completed tasks. This helps evaluate their effectiveness and accuracy.

outsourcing.title: 6. Working with external contractors
outsourcing.content:
Sometimes certain actions can be assigned to external contractors. Since we do not control an external contractor, we can only define the moment when the task is handed over for execution and the moment when the result is received from the contractor.

Accordingly, you need to add the external contractor as a unit, but mark them as external (outer). If we want to assign an action to an external contractor, we simply drag this action onto their lane in the planning field and specify their contractual time frame (by dragging or via the context menu).

In the monitor, you can see a list of tasks assigned to external contractors (Monitor → External actions). There, at the moment of acceptance of the result, it is marked whether the task has been completed or a defect has been detected. After that, the standard defect handling and replanning process takes place.

resources.title: 7. Program Settings
resources.content:
7.1. Team

The team has one administrator — who is also the master and team owner. During registration, they create the team, receive its number, and become the manager. The administrator creates and plans cards, has access to reports, receives service invoices, and communicates with support.

Other users enter the team number during registration. The administrator assigns them roles and can remove or deactivate a member.

7.2. Schedule

The team operates according to a shared schedule, based on the specified location. You can configure work hours, breaks, weekends, and holidays (always considered non-working). Additional working hours can be set for specific days.

Important: This setting does not affect the time display on the timeline; it is configured separately.

7.3. Actions

A catalog of all possible operations is configured: assembly, disassembly, painting, etc. Each operation has a unique code and an interruptibility parameter that affects planning.

7.4. Units of Measurement

A catalog of all units of measurement used in tech cards. Each unit has a unique code for synchronization with other systems.

7.5. Units

A unit is a performer: a worker, a machine, or a team. It can be of type "Control" (controller) or "Process" (executor). It may also be an external contractor.

Each unit has a setup time parameter — the time required for preparation. A task performance coefficient (qualification factor) is also specified. Individual deviations from the common schedule can be set and will be considered during planning.

7.6. Settings

Includes:

  • Calendar settings: display of time, weekends, and holidays on the timeline.
  • Accounting settings: whether there is a controller in the team.

7.7. Templates

Contains an editable list of tech card templates for faster entry of repeat orders.

quickstart.title: 8. Quick Start
quickstart.content:
8.1. Initial Setup

After registration, the master creates a team and defines basic parameters: work schedule, holidays, breaks, and uploads catalogs of available actions and units of measurement. This is necessary for proper planning functionality.

8.2. Assigning Roles to Users

The master shares the team number with other users. They enter it during registration. Then, in the “Team” panel, the master assigns roles — designating participants as performers and a controller (if needed).

8.3. First Process Card

Create a tech card in the “Cards” panel by clicking “Add.” Start by adding the product you want to produce, define the sequence of actions, and specify necessary materials for each action. The program will automatically determine the list of final materials.

8.4. Planning

After completing the editing, click “Send for planning.” Don’t forget to save your changes. Go to the planning panel and drag the card to the timeline. The program will automatically assign performers based on their workload and schedule. You can manually adjust the performer and operation time if necessary.

8.5. Execution Monitoring

Performers receive tasks in the form of a daily schedule (visible in Monitor → Unit Load). Each task contains a description, list of materials, expected result, and deadline. After completing the task, the performer presses the “Completed” button or “Ready” / “Defect” depending on control settings. The master can view the real-time status of all tasks in the planning panel and reports.

8.6. Closing the Card

Once all tasks are completed, including any defect corrections, the card receives the “Ready” status. The master can then close it, and it will disappear from the planning panel. If necessary, the card can be saved as a JSON file for future use as a template.
